text: my jacket over a chair and retreated to a steamer trunk. I undid the cast iron latches and carefully opened the top, revealing a set of sanitized surgical tools and heavy-duty, shoulder length rubber gloves. “Holy shit.” Vanessa traced a finger across my neck, sending a shiver through me. “I’m so fucking turned on right now.” # The waterway which skirted the edge of campus was called Forgotten Cove. The location was famous for two things. One was its relative proximity to Eskuatep University. The second was for being the site of countless missing persons. From what I could tell, investigations never got far. Had Silvia Tull walked naked and wailing into Forgotten Cove as a thick mist swallowed her from sight? Had Reginald Davis eaten a Black Sea Bass pulled from the murky waters, leading to a strange illness and his eventual disappearance? Did the black sands come alive one predawn morning and envelop Old Gus Warner as he loaded his fishing boat, stealing him away to unknown shores? I didn’t know. These people had surely vanished from the area, but the details were no more than quietly whispered gossip among the locals. Residents of Delhyme loved to talk, but nothing they said struck true. They were simply extensions of the ghost stories that drew me there in the first place. When I asked questions, tried to find the primary sources of the stories, I’d invariably hit a dead end. That is, until I met Benny Dickson. Another outsider like me. Someone who saw past the hastily laid camouflage and poorly executed subterfuge. Someone who had answers. # While Vanessa played with the surgical tools, I set up the staging area in my living room, placing yards of plastic sheeting. In the middle, I placed a wood chair sourced from Delhyme, made by a carpenter using local oak. It was sturdy and only looked better with age and staining. “You almost ready?” She stomped a foot like an adolescent waiting for a promised ice cream cone. “Almost.” I removed the lid to the container. A strong smell of sea water and fertilizer escaped along with a gurgling squeal. “Bring me a scalpel.” This time the squeal was one of excitement issued by Vanessa. She rummaged through the tools and jogged over to the counter. Vanessa jabbed the blade at me. “Now?” “Whoa, careful.” I held my hand out. Vanessa frowned theatrically and handed me the scalpel. “What is that?” She leaned over the container and waved a hand in front of her nose. “Gross. Smells like rotting fish heads.” “Can you tell what’s water and what isn’t?” Holding her nose, she inspected what looked like undulating liquid. “Um.” “Check this out.” I poked the surface with the handle of the scalpel, withdrawing quickly. The creature in the water became visible as it flashed blue and red lights along thin tendrils connected to a bulbous body. Tiny groups of spheres, like miniature grape clusters, hung from floating tentacles the size of threads. Other tubular protrusions connected to a set of wings. “What—?” “Bioluminescence,” I said. Vanessa dropped her hand. “But what is it?” She turned, eyes wide. “And why do we have it?” “You said on your profile that you like to play nurse.” I handed the scalpel back to her. “And I need an assistant.” # Summer on a college campus can be quiet, even eerie. But Eskuatep was something else. One hot morning, I walked to campus, intending to explore the cove. The builders of Eskuatep managed to completely block access except by walking through campus. The walkways were made of the same white stone I’d seen around town. The same as buildings, or at least similar to my untrained eyes. Ponderous trees and hedges grew along the paths, fencing them in and making navigation difficult. Harder still due to lack of signs or maps. It was impossible to stay on a single path, as the walkway forked and forked again. A tall building loomed ahead. More tower than schoolhouse. One leg of the path led to the front of the building, but there were no doors. I walked clockwise, to the adjoining wall and found only vine-covered stone. A dizziness overcame me as I continued to circle, finding the third wall as bare as the first. No doors. No windows for that matter. I leaned on my knees, exhausted for no reason. My head buzzed with summer sounds. The coastal scents became oppressive. I turned until I saw a break in the trees where the coast must be, then headed in that direction. But the paths did not cooperate. With the overgrown vegetation on either side, it was like being trapped in a hedge maze. The pathways zigged and zagged off course, branching into various directions, none of which suited me. I heard footsteps behind me, echoing as if in a long hallway. I saw no one, but increased my speed, nonetheless. I chose a path. Then another. I should have wanted to find the person, ask them how in God’s name to escape the labyrinth. But my gut told me no . My gut told me to run . And once the slippery word got inside, with terror mounting, my body reacted independent of logic. The wind blew a rancid stench of rotting fish, so concentrated my eyes teared. Footsteps grew louder behind me. My mind screamed to run faster, and I did. I ran until I saw a tall building with no doors. I kept running until I passed a familiar set of paths, and another unmarked building. How big was this campus? Why did the monolithic buildings defy reason? I stopped and checked the skyline. The coast was behind me. Footsteps continued. In a fit of panic, I jumped headfirst through a hedge. Thorns drew thin lines of blood across my face and arms until I tumbled into a stand of oak and pine. Free of the dreaded stone pathway, my head cleared of
